note_html:
Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Larry, has been experiencing chronic back pain for the past couple of years, which initially started after moving furniture one weekend. The pain is described as a sharp stabbing pain in the back that radiates down to the right leg, specifically to the big toe. He also reports numbness and tingling in the right leg, particularly in the foot, and difficulty getting comfortable, especially when sitting. He denies any loss of sensation in the genital or rectal area, weakness, loss of bladder or bowel control, and has not experienced any numbness or tingling in the past.

Past medical history: The patient has a history of trying physical therapy and strong pain medications in the past, which provided temporary relief. He has also been considered for surgery but would like to avoid it.

Review of systems: The patient reports difficulty with activity due to back pain, which has led to weight gain.

Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]

Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]

Physical exam: On musculoskeletal examination, there is no overlying erythema or ecchymosis, but there is midline tenderness at the L4-L5 disk space with right-sided lumbar paravertebral tenderness. The patient experiences pain with lumbar flexion and a positive straight leg test. On neurological examination, there is decreased sensation to light touch at the right lateral thigh at L4 dermatome, and patellar and Achilles reflexes are symmetrical.

Test Results: The MRI shows disk herniation with nerve impingement.

Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient's symptoms are consistent with an L4-L5 herniated disk with radiculopathy, indicating a bulging disk that is pinching on some nerves, causing tingling in the leg.

Problem list: L4-L5 herniated disk with radiculopathy; chronic lumbar back pain; radiculopathy.

Plan: I recommended a spinal injection with a corticosteroid, specifically a strong inflammatory medication, to help reduce inflammation and alleviate pain. The injection will be performed under fluoroscopy, and the patient will be monitored for response to treatment. The patient will not have to undergo more than four injections per year, and the procedure is relatively quick, taking about an hour.

Follow-up: The patient will be scheduled for the first injection today, and subsequent injections will be scheduled as needed, based on response to treatment.
